steps.conclusion: The modern toolchain is missing version control and collaborative change workflows such as Git, branches, pull requests, code review, and merge-conflict handling. This is a notable junior-practitioner gap. The closing chapter also does not clearly cover contribution and entry paths—jobs, communities, and how to begin contributing to open source—even though it covers roles, portfolio evidence, and staying current.

Nearly every description follows the repetitive “Covers…” and “You will…” formula, making the otherwise welcoming outline feel mechanical. The repeated “You will” outcomes can also read as claims about learner performance. Sequencing could be smoother: package management would naturally precede building Node.js servers, while the substantial algorithms and advanced metaprogramming material could come after learners have built more browser applications.

Add a subject-specific chapter on Git and collaborative JavaScript development, and expand the closing chapter with concrete entry routes and community participation. More small integrated projects between the DOM, Node.js, testing, and tooling chapters would strengthen the progression toward the two excellent end-to-end workflows.

- MUST be in US English

      
  - Mission context: this curriculum is for learners OUTSIDE privileged environments — people who doubt they can become serious practitioners. Chapters should feel welcoming and legible, not gate-kept by academic vocabulary
  - MAJOR ERROR: the curriculum must be a serious arc from zero knowledge to mastery, not a shallow tour or a list of disconnected topics
  - MAJOR ERROR: chapters must follow a logical learning progression. Each chapter should depend only on earlier chapters and ordinary life experience a true beginner can reasonably have
  - MAJOR ERROR: the opening chapters must teach the natural foundations a true beginner needs first
  - MAJOR ERROR: practical work must appear throughout the course once prerequisites support it. A curriculum that stays purely theoretical or avoids projects, tools, cases, techniques, artifacts, and workflows is incomplete
  - MAJOR ERROR: opening with a showcase project, specialist case, lab technique, advanced tool, or dramatic scenario before the learner has the prerequisites is wrong even when the example is real and concrete
  - For chronological subjects, the chapter order must follow the real chronology. For skill-based subjects, it must follow the real skill progression. For science and engineering, observable foundations should come before specialist techniques
  - Foundational chapters are allowed and often necessary, but they must teach concrete concepts, vocabulary, chronology, notation, mental models, or simple skills the learner will use later — not just describe the field from the outside
  - Chapter count is a RESULT of coverage, not a target. Do not penalize or reward specific counts when coverage is justified. Evaluate whether every canonical pillar of the field has at least one chapter — if any pillar is missing, the curriculum is incomplete regardless of count. Broad fields, large platforms, and large fictional canons can legitimately need many chapters. Penalize duplicate, padded, or poorly sequenced chapters, not length by itself
  - MAJOR ERROR: trading breadth for recency. Modern topics must ADD coverage without REPLACING canonical foundations. For example, a machine learning curriculum that covers transformers/LLMs/RAG/diffusion/fine-tuning but drops reinforcement learning, time-series forecasting, recommender systems, or causal inference is incomplete — those are canonical ML pillars. A medicine curriculum that covers telemedicine and modern EMRs but drops anatomy, physiology, pathology, or pharmacology is incomplete. If covering everything needs more chapters, more chapters is the correct answer
  - Must cover BOTH academic AND practical pillars:
    - Academic pillars: theory, foundations, history, canonical body of knowledge
    - Modern toolchain: what working practitioners in this field actually use this year
    - End-to-end workflow: at least one chapter walking through how real work in the field gets delivered start to finish
    - Last-decade shifts: what's been added to the field in the past 10 years that a textbook wouldn't have covered 10 years ago (new regulations, new platforms, new techniques, AI changes, etc.)
  - MAJOR ERROR: for broad fields, professions, sciences, engineering subjects, and serious practices, missing a standalone history/evolution chapter is an academic-pillar gap unless the course itself is already chronological history or the subject is a narrow tool/hobby where history would be padding
  - MAJOR ERROR: for fields, professions, and serious practices, missing a standalone end-to-end workflow chapter is a practical-pillar gap. The workflow should show real work from problem/request/question through decisions, execution, validation, delivery, operation, maintenance, or follow-up, depending on the field
  - For regulated professions and fields with safety duties, local rules, required documents, reporting duties, professional responsibility, and safety practice are curriculum pillars. A generic ethics chapter is not enough when practitioners need separate procedures and judgment
  - The "junior practitioner test": if a junior would be embarrassed on day 1 of real practice because a topic is missing, that topic must exist as its own chapter
  - Cover core pillars before specialized or frontier topics
  - Stay tightly focused on the course title. Generic cross-disciplinary chapters that could be copied unchanged into many unrelated courses are a major error unless the topic IS the subject of the course
  - MAJOR ERROR: meta-chapters that describe, taxonomize, or survey the field from the outside instead of teaching it
  - MAJOR ERROR: survey chapters that list many areas as bullet points without teaching any of them — each topic that matters deserves its own chapter with real depth
  - MAJOR ERROR: catch-all "recent developments" chapters. Titles like "Catch up with the last decade of X", "What's new in X", "Recent developments in X", "The last decade of X", or "Modern X" that bundle multiple distinct modern techniques into a single chapter are a major error. Each modern shift (transformers, LLMs, RAG, diffusion, fine-tuning for ML; CRISPR, sequencing, single-cell biology, spatial omics, synthetic biology for Biology; LGPD/GDPR, digital filing for Law; telemedicine, EMRs for Medicine; etc.) deserves its own substantive chapter
  - Avoid overlap. If two chapters would naturally share most of their lessons, they should be merged or their scopes sharpened
  - Prefer concept names over vendor names in chapter titles/descriptions (e.g., "Package management" instead of a specific tool name). Foundational tools that ARE the subject of the course are an allowed exception. Vendor name usage in non-vendor courses is a minor issue, never a major error
  - For courses covering a field, profession, or serious practice, a "navigating the field" closing chapter is expected. It must cover ROLES AND SPECIALTIES (what kinds of practitioners exist and what they do differently), CONTRIBUTION/ENTRY PATHS (how people actually join — jobs, residencies, open source, communities), the ARTIFACT THAT PROVES COMPETENCE in this field (code repos, case records, clinical hours, recordings), and HOW PRACTITIONERS KEEP SKILLS CURRENT. A narrow "Build a Portfolio" or "Build a Project" closing chapter is a major error — a portfolio is just one small part of navigating a field. The closing chapter must be specific to the subject, not generic career advice
  - For hobby / pop-culture topics, no closing navigation chapter is expected
  - Titles: concise, specific, concrete, natural, learner-facing, and sentence case. Preserve normal capitalization for proper nouns, product names, acronyms, and language-specific conventions. Chapter titles should use the canonical names a serious learner would expect in a normal course outline, especially for searchable field pillars, methods, tools, eras, genres, regulations, standards, and named concepts. Canonical titles such as "Algorithms", "Data structures", "Operating systems", "Cell biology", "Civil procedure", "Backpropagation", "Retrieval-augmented generation", or their natural translated equivalents are good when those are the real chapter topics
  - MAJOR STYLE ISSUE: hiding canonical terms behind practical paraphrases, metaphors, slogans, or "what it does" titles. If a learner scanning or searching for a standard topic would not recognize the chapter because the title avoids the expected term, the title is not good enough. For example, "Informação guardada em bits" is worse than a title that names "Representação de dados"; "O que um computador faz de verdade" is worse than a title that names the actual pillar such as "Arquitetura de computadores" or "Organização de computadores"; "Catch bugs before users do" is worse than "Testing JavaScript code"
  - SCORE CAP: if 3 or more chapter titles hide important canonical/searchable terms that belong in those titles, the majorErrors score must be 7 or lower even when descriptions mention the terms and the underlying coverage is strong. If 1-2 important titles hide canonical/searchable terms, the majorErrors score must be 8 or lower. Descriptions recovering the term do not remove the title problem because users scan and search chapter titles first
  - MAJOR STYLE ISSUE: titles that read like academic catalog entries only because they are bloated, comma-separated inventories, abstract noun stacks, or title-case headlines. Do not penalize a concise canonical course title as "academic" just because it uses the standard term for the topic. Avoid vague, cute, clickbait, or slogan-like titles such as "X Without Fear" unless that phrase is the natural name of the topic. Avoid "I / II / Part 1" — use descriptive subtitles instead
  - Descriptions: 1–2 sentences describing what the chapter covers and, when natural, what it enables. Descriptions should explain the chapter's content clearly enough that a learner can tell which canonical topics, tools, cases, mechanisms, or skills belong inside it. A description that only says what the learner will feel or broadly do is too vague if it does not name the actual scope
  - Description filler issue: descriptions should not rely on filler phrases like "explore", "understand", "learn about", "introduction to", or "basics of". Repeated filler phrasing, or descriptions that use these words instead of naming concrete scope, is a minor issue.
  - Warm, plain language throughout. Avoid academic vocabulary
  - Language output must follow the language parameter. For `en`: US English. For `pt`: Brazilian Portuguese. For `es`: Latin American Spanish
  - Titles and descriptions must not leak prompt instructions or make claims about the learner's performance
  - Don't evaluate output format — focus on chapter content quality only

The progression is strong overall, but advanced probabilistic forecasting and Monte Carlo appear before core system-design material such as Kanban cadences, STATIK, and the end-to-end workflow. The output also mixes avoidable English into Brazilian Portuguese, such as “lead time distribution,” “aging WIP,” and “Forecasting probabilístico,” and lists several vendors in the digital-tools description.

Move cadences, STATIK, system design, and the complete service workflow ahead of advanced forecasting. Prefer Brazilian Portuguese formulations such as “previsão probabilística” and provide Portuguese explanations for unavoidable field-standard English terms. The closing chapter could also state entry paths more concretely, such as internal improvement initiatives, junior flow roles, communities of practice, consulting apprenticeships, and evidence from measured service improvements.

steps.conclusion: The curriculum is exceptionally broad and well sequenced, but it still omits a few canonical pillars expected in a zero-to-mastery Brazilian Law course. In particular, Juizados Especiais appear only incidentally inside online dispute resolution rather than receiving substantive standalone coverage, and core academic perspectives such as Filosofia do Direito and Sociologia do Direito are absent. Dedicated treatment of constitutional remedies and administrative procedure is also too implicit for their practical importance in Brazil.

Descriptions are concrete and useful, but their highly repetitive openings—especially “Cobre” and “Trata”—make the curriculum feel formulaic. A few English labels, such as “Legal design”, “Legal operations” and “compliance”, could be paired with established Portuguese equivalents for greater accessibility, though these terms are commonly used in Brazilian practice.

The course could be improved by slightly varying description structure and by making the final career chapter more explicit about the competence artifacts appropriate to different legal paths, such as supervised practice records, case analyses, research, drafted instruments and compliance projects—not only a portfolio of legal filings.

steps.conclusion: The dedicated biosafety chapter is sequenced too late: learners encounter pipetting, biological samples, microbial collection/culture, and fieldwork before receiving the course’s substantive treatment of risk assessment, containment, PPE, transport, waste, and accident response. It should precede practical microbiology and other sample-handling workflows. In addition, the zero-to-mastery arc lacks a later substantive biostatistics chapter: the early coverage stops at means, variation, error, and simple graphs, without statistical inference, confidence intervals, regression, hypothesis testing, effect sizes, or power.

“Assortimento independente” is an avoidable English-influenced expression; “segregação independente” is the standard Brazilian Portuguese term. The closing chapter covers roles, entry paths, professional bodies, and staying current, but merely names a portfolio without specifying biology-specific evidence of competence such as field/lab notebooks, protocols, datasets, analysis repositories, technical reports, or documented collections. “Biologia” should normally be lowercase in the final title.

Add clearer coverage of major plant lineages and life cycles rather than leaving them implicit in a broad diversity chapter. Renaming “Imagem biológica moderna” to a more searchable title such as “Microscopia avançada e análise de imagens biológicas” would also expose the canonical method more clearly. Otherwise, the curriculum is unusually comprehensive, practical, welcoming, and strong across canonical and modern biology.

steps.conclusion: The required structural-simulation coverage is incomplete. The dedicated finite element analysis chapter covers general FEA concepts and mentions chassis and suspension parts, but it does not explicitly teach composite modeling, crash-structure/impact simulation, or simulation-led lightweight part design. Separate chapters on composites, crash safety, and mass management do not establish the required FEA connection. This is a material gap in an explicit course requirement.

Several titles and descriptions rely unnecessarily on English terms despite the Brazilian Portuguese requirement, including “Workflow de desenvolvimento de performance,” “assemblies,” “fail-safe,” “driver-in-the-loop,” “make-or-buy,” “spares” and “turnaround.” Some are common industry vocabulary, but Portuguese wording or immediate clarification would make the curriculum more welcoming. The history chapter is also somewhat compressed: its description promises the right scope but does not name key eras and regulatory or safety milestones clearly enough to demonstrate substantial coverage.

Expand structural simulation into explicit, substantive coverage of composite laminates and failure criteria, crash boxes and nonlinear impact analysis, chassis and suspension validation, and topology/shape optimization for lightweight parts. Strengthen the history chapter by naming major eras and transitions, such as front- to rear-engined layouts, ground effect, turbo eras, active suspension and its ban, refueling and tire-rule changes, hybrid power units, halo and crash-test evolution, the 2022 ground-effect rules, cost caps, and the 2026 transition. Add more concrete learner artifacts to practical chapters, such as CAD releases, FEA reports, correlation notebooks, setup sheets, test plans and post-session engineering reports.

steps.conclusion: None. The curriculum provides a serious beginner-to-advanced progression, includes concrete practice throughout, covers Google Cloud’s major architectural pillars, gives Cloud Spanner standalone treatment, and includes both history and end-to-end workflow chapters.

A few current-product details need updating. “Cloud Functions” should acknowledge the current Cloud Run functions naming and the relationship between generations. Introduce observability before the GKE operations chapter, which currently relies on logging and monitoring concepts taught much later. A dedicated Google Cloud Architecture Framework chapter would also strengthen explicit coverage of architecture principles, tradeoffs, and design review practices.

steps.conclusion: The chronology has a material sequencing error: Dragon Ball Super: Broly belongs directly after the Tournament of Power and before the Moro and Granolah arcs, but it is placed after both. This conflicts with the required chronological progression and can confuse learners about modern canon.

The original manga’s epilogue, including Uub and the final World Martial Arts Tournament, is not clearly covered despite its importance to later continuity. There is also a capitalization typo in “trunks from the future,” which should be “Trunks.”

Move the Broly chapter before the Galactic Patrol Prisoner saga, explicitly include the End of Z/Uub material, and clarify coverage of the Super Hero manga prequel/adaptation and the unresolved Black Frieza thread. These changes would make the otherwise broad, welcoming, practical curriculum more complete and chronologically dependable.

steps.conclusion: The curriculum has a substantial prerequisite gap for a true zero-to-mastery path. It moves from only brief foundations in units, radiation, matter, and mechanics into photometry, spectroscopy, cosmology, compact objects, and numerical simulations without dedicated mathematical/statistical foundations or sufficient thermodynamics, atomic/nuclear physics, and relativity. General relativity appears explicitly only in the late gravitational-wave chapter, even though compact remnants, gravitational lensing, active galactic nuclei, and Friedmann-Lemaître cosmology rely on it earlier. In addition, specialist observing techniques such as interferometry and high-energy astronomy are placed before core object-based astronomy such as the Solar System, stars, and galaxies. The dedicated history chapter, end-to-end observational workflow, practical work, modern developments, and career-navigation chapter otherwise satisfy major requirements.

A few titles emphasize particular tools or facilities rather than durable concepts, notably “Python, Astropy y archivos FITS” and “Astronomía infrarroja y JWST.” The history description is also somewhat compressed and vague about early modern astronomy, spectroscopy, relativity, and the discovery of cosmic expansion, although those areas are substantially recovered in other chapters as the rubric permits.

Add early chapters on mathematics for astronomy, probability and statistics, and the physical foundations of astrophysics, followed by a dedicated relativity chapter before relativistic objects and cosmology. Move the core Solar System, stellar, galactic, and cosmological sequence ahead of the more specialized wavelength and interferometric techniques, or explicitly frame those techniques after the relevant objects and physics. The history chapter could name the ancient-to-geocentric-to-heliocentric transition, telescopic astronomy, spectroscopy, relativity, expansion, and modern multiwavelength/space observation more clearly.

steps.conclusion: Several explicit curriculum requirements are materially unmet. Major traditions are still compressed into survey-style catch-alls: “Música brasileña” bundles samba, choro, bossa nova, tropicalia, MPB and forró; “Músicas del Caribe latino” combines son, salsa, timba, merengue, bachata and plena; “Tradiciones de China, Japón y Corea” merges several distinct national traditions; and “Música de club y cultura DJ” compresses disco, house, techno, trance, drum and bass and EDM. Likewise, most instrument areas receive only one inventory-heavy chapter, so the practical sequence does not plausibly progress from beginner technique to mastery on an instrument. The closing chapter covers roles, portfolio artifacts and staying current, but does not adequately explain concrete entry paths such as auditions, session work, employment, community ensembles or professional associations.

Many descriptions are dense lists of unexplained specialist terms, which weakens the welcoming, beginner-legible mission. Examples include “slash chords,” “hooks,” “stems,” “spotting,” “middleware,” “pitching editorial” and “venue”; some are normal professional vocabulary, but they should be translated or briefly contextualized. A few chapters also span too many techniques or instruments to communicate realistic depth, particularly the grouped strings, woodwinds, brass and percussion chapters.

Split the bundled regional and popular traditions into meaningful historical eras, genre families or practice areas. Add progressive beginner, intermediate and advanced pathways for at least a chosen primary instrument or voice, with repertoire, technique milestones, ensemble work and assessed performances. Sharpen the final navigation chapter with concrete routes into paid and community practice, expected professional artifacts, mentorship and audition pathways. The otherwise strong production, live-performance, legal, workflow and recent-technology coverage should be retained.

steps.conclusion: The closing chapter does not fully meet the required field-navigation and end-to-end workflow pillars. It lists possible paths and asks learners to build an argument, but it does not explain historian roles and specialties, concrete entry routes, competence artifacts, skill maintenance, or a research workflow from question formation through source collection, validation, synthesis, citation, and delivery. Also, the final contemporary chapter hides the searchable canonical event name “January 8 attacks” in its description behind the broader title “defense of democracy,” triggering the title score cap.

There is a localized chronological reversal: the abolition chapter reaches the Golden Law of 1888 before the curriculum returns to the Paraguayan War of 1864–1870. The Dilma chapter and the following Operation Car Wash chapter also overlap substantially and would benefit from sharper boundaries.

Retitle the final contemporary chapter to name the January 8 attacks directly. Split the last chapter into a concrete historical-research workflow and a subject-specific navigation chapter covering academic history, public history, archives, museums, teaching, and journalism, including entry paths, evidence of competence, and ways practitioners remain current.

steps.conclusion: The curriculum is exceptionally broad and modern, but it does not fully support a true beginner from ordinary-life experience: the Python chapter jumps directly into notebooks, arrays, tables, and datasets without clearly teaching programming fundamentals such as variables, types, conditionals, loops, functions, imports, and debugging. That missing prerequisite can block nearly every practical chapter. Reinforcement learning is also represented only by a conceptual foundations chapter; a mastery curriculum should include core algorithms and practical training, such as value-based and policy-based methods.

Some early sequencing is awkward. Cross-entropy and empirical risk appear before probability and statistics, while optimization appears before problem framing and the main supervised models that make its purpose concrete. Statistical learning theory also interrupts the practical classical-model progression and would be more accessible after learners have encountered more model families.

Add a genuine Python programming foundations chapter, move loss functions after the supporting math, and place advanced learning theory later. Expand reinforcement learning into an applied chapter covering algorithms, evaluation, simulation, and modern deep RL. The rest is highly useful: it includes canonical foundations, history, practical projects, modern architectures, LLMs, RAG, fine-tuning, diffusion models, production infrastructure, governance, end-to-end workflows, and field navigation with clear canonical titles.

  - MAJOR ERROR: practical work must appear throughout the course once prerequisites support it. A curriculum that stays purely theoretical or avoids projects, tools, cases, techniques, artifacts, and workflows is incomplete
  - MAJOR ERROR: opening with a showcase project, specialist case, lab technique, advanced tool, or dramatic scenario before the learner has the prerequisites is wrong even when the example is real and concrete
  - For chronological subjects, the chapter order must follow the real chronology. For skill-based subjects, it must follow the real skill progression. For science and engineering, observable foundations should come before specialist techniques
  - Foundational chapters are allowed and often necessary, but they must teach concrete concepts, vocabulary, chronology, notation, mental models, or simple skills the learner will use later — not just describe the field from the outside
  - Chapter count is a RESULT of coverage, not a target. Do not penalize or reward specific counts when coverage is justified. Evaluate whether every canonical pillar of the field has at least one chapter — if any pillar is missing, the curriculum is incomplete regardless of count. Broad fields, large platforms, and large fictional canons can legitimately need many chapters. Penalize duplicate, padded, or poorly sequenced chapters, not length by itself
  - MAJOR ERROR: trading breadth for recency. Modern topics must ADD coverage without REPLACING canonical foundations. For example, a machine learning curriculum that covers transformers/LLMs/RAG/diffusion/fine-tuning but drops reinforcement learning, time-series forecasting, recommender systems, or causal inference is incomplete — those are canonical ML pillars. A medicine curriculum that covers telemedicine and modern EMRs but drops anatomy, physiology, pathology, or pharmacology is incomplete. If covering everything needs more chapters, more chapters is the correct answer
  - Must cover BOTH academic AND practical pillars:
    - Academic pillars: theory, foundations, history, canonical body of knowledge
    - Modern toolchain: what working practitioners in this field actually use this year
    - End-to-end workflow: at least one chapter walking through how real work in the field gets delivered start to finish
    - Last-decade shifts: what's been added to the field in the past 10 years that a textbook wouldn't have covered 10 years ago (new regulations, new platforms, new techniques, AI changes, etc.)
  - MAJOR ERROR: for broad fields, professions, sciences, engineering subjects, and serious practices, missing a standalone history/evolution chapter is an academic-pillar gap unless the course itself is already chronological history or the subject is a narrow tool/hobby where history would be padding
  - MAJOR ERROR: for fields, professions, and serious practices, missing a standalone end-to-end workflow chapter is a practical-pillar gap. The workflow should show real work from problem/request/question through decisions, execution, validation, delivery, operation, maintenance, or follow-up, depending on the field
  - For regulated professions and fields with safety duties, local rules, required documents, reporting duties, professional responsibility, and safety practice are curriculum pillars. A generic ethics chapter is not enough when practitioners need separate procedures and judgment
  - The "junior practitioner test": if a junior would be embarrassed on day 1 of real practice because a topic is missing, that topic must exist as its own chapter
  - Cover core pillars before specialized or frontier topics
  - Stay tightly focused on the course title. Generic cross-disciplinary chapters that could be copied unchanged into many unrelated courses are a major error unless the topic IS the subject of the course
  - MAJOR ERROR: meta-chapters that describe, taxonomize, or survey the field from the outside instead of teaching it
  - MAJOR ERROR: survey chapters that list many areas as bullet points without teaching any of them — each topic that matters deserves its own chapter with real depth
  - MAJOR ERROR: catch-all "recent developments" chapters. Titles like "Catch up with the last decade of X", "What's new in X", "Recent developments in X", "The last decade of X", or "Modern X" that bundle multiple distinct modern techniques into a single chapter are a major error. Each modern shift (transformers, LLMs, RAG, diffusion, fine-tuning for ML; CRISPR, sequencing, single-cell biology, spatial omics, synthetic biology for Biology; LGPD/GDPR, digital filing for Law; telemedicine, EMRs for Medicine; etc.) deserves its own substantive chapter
  - Avoid overlap. If two chapters would naturally share most of their lessons, they should be merged or their scopes sharpened
  - Prefer concept names over vendor names in chapter titles/descriptions (e.g., "Package management" instead of a specific tool name). Foundational tools that ARE the subject of the course are an allowed exception. Vendor name usage in non-vendor courses is a minor issue, never a major error
  - For courses covering a field, profession, or serious practice, a "navigating the field" closing chapter is expected. It must cover ROLES AND SPECIALTIES (what kinds of practitioners exist and what they do differently), CONTRIBUTION/ENTRY PATHS (how people actually join — jobs, residencies, open source, communities), the ARTIFACT THAT PROVES COMPETENCE in this field (code repos, case records, clinical hours, recordings), and HOW PRACTITIONERS KEEP SKILLS CURRENT. A narrow "Build a Portfolio" or "Build a Project" closing chapter is a major error — a portfolio is just one small part of navigating a field. The closing chapter must be specific to the subject, not generic career advice
  - For hobby / pop-culture topics, no closing navigation chapter is expected
  - Titles: concise, specific, concrete, natural, learner-facing, and sentence case. Preserve normal capitalization for proper nouns, product names, acronyms, and language-specific conventions. Chapter titles should use the canonical names a serious learner would expect in a normal course outline, especially for searchable field pillars, methods, tools, eras, genres, regulations, standards, and named concepts. Canonical titles such as "Algorithms", "Data structures", "Operating systems", "Cell biology", "Civil procedure", "Backpropagation", "Retrieval-augmented generation", or their natural translated equivalents are good when those are the real chapter topics
  - MAJOR STYLE ISSUE: hiding canonical terms behind practical paraphrases, metaphors, slogans, or "what it does" titles. If a learner scanning or searching for a standard topic would not recognize the chapter because the title avoids the expected term, the title is not good enough. For example, "Informação guardada em bits" is worse than a title that names "Representação de dados"; "O que um computador faz de verdade" is worse than a title that names the actual pillar such as "Arquitetura de computadores" or "Organização de computadores"; "Catch bugs before users do" is worse than "Testing JavaScript code"
  - SCORE CAP: if 3 or more chapter titles hide important canonical/searchable terms that belong in those titles, the majorErrors score must be 7 or lower even when descriptions mention the terms and the underlying coverage is strong. If 1-2 important titles hide canonical/searchable terms, the majorErrors score must be 8 or lower. Descriptions recovering the term do not remove the title problem because users scan and search chapter titles first
  - MAJOR STYLE ISSUE: titles that read like academic catalog entries only because they are bloated, comma-separated inventories, abstract noun stacks, or title-case headlines. Do not penalize a concise canonical course title as "academic" just because it uses the standard term for the topic. Avoid vague, cute, clickbait, or slogan-like titles such as "X Without Fear" unless that phrase is the natural name of the topic. Avoid "I / II / Part 1" — use descriptive subtitles instead
  - Descriptions: 1–2 sentences describing what the chapter covers and, when natural, what it enables. Descriptions should explain the chapter's content clearly enough that a learner can tell which canonical topics, tools, cases, mechanisms, or skills belong inside it. A description that only says what the learner will feel or broadly do is too vague if it does not name the actual scope
  - Description filler issue: descriptions should not rely on filler phrases like "explore", "understand", "learn about", "introduction to", or "basics of". Repeated filler phrasing, or descriptions that use these words instead of naming concrete scope, is a minor issue.
  - Warm, plain language throughout. Avoid academic vocabulary
  - Language output must follow the language parameter. For `en`: US English. For `pt`: Brazilian Portuguese. For `es`: Latin American Spanish
  - Titles and descriptions must not leak prompt instructions or make claims about the learner's performance
  - Don't evaluate output format — focus on chapter content quality only
